title: Molecular Dynamics simulations of Al-Ti metallic alloy melts using a transferable machine-learning potential
authors: Yuna Kato, Jürgen Brillo, Dirk Holland-Moritz, Fan Yang, Thomas C. Hansen, Thomas Voigtmann, Linnea Heitmeier
abstract: We investigate the structural and dynamical properties of binary aluminum-titanium liquid metallic alloys, as a function of temperature and composition. We make use of MD-simulations, using a transferable machine-learning potential developed by Song et al. [Nature Communications 15, 10208 (2024)], and compare our results to experimental data. Although this potential was initially trained on solid properties, we find good agreement between the experimental data and the simulation results for the liquid state. The excess volume and compositional changes of the structure are captured well by the machine-learned potential. The simulation allows to disentangle local packing from chemical-ordering effects; the latter are found to be weak in Al-Ti. Dynamical quantities like the viscosity and the diffusion coefficients are also discussed.
summary: This paper demonstrates that a machine-learning potential trained on solid-state data can accurately simulate the properties of liquid Al-Ti alloys. The researchers successfully modeled density, structure, and viscosity, providing new predictions for diffusion coefficients. However, they identified significant breakdowns in the Darken approximation and the Stokes-Einstein relation in Al-rich melts. This work highlights both the power of transferable ML potentials and the complexity of transport dynamics in multicomponent metallic liquids.
main_problem: The study aims to validate the transferability of a machine-learning interatomic potential (NEP89) to liquid Al-Ti alloys and to characterize their structural and dynamical properties across various temperatures and compositions.
main_result: The ML potential accurately reproduces experimental density, structure factors, and viscosity, though it fails to capture the Darken approximation and Stokes-Einstein relation in Al-rich regimes due to decoupling of tracer dynamics.
method: Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations using the Neuro-Evolution Potential (NEP89) in the LAMMPS software, benchmarked against experimental neutron diffraction and electromagnetic levitation data.
model_or_system: A binary aluminum-titanium (Al-Ti) liquid metallic alloy system studied in the temperature range of 1550 K to 2200 K with aluminum mole fractions ranging from 0.0 to 1.0.
key_observables: Mass density, molar volume, excess volume, pair-correlation functions (PCF), static structure factor S(q), viscosity, self-diffusion coefficients, and inter-diffusion coefficients.
important_parameters: Temperature (1550-2200 K), Aluminum mole fraction (x_Al from 0.0 to 1.0), and the thermodynamic factor (Phi).
main_assumptions: The machine-learning potential, originally trained on solid-state properties, is assumed to be transferable to the liquid state; the use of the soft-sphere model as a reference for local packing.
figures_summary: Figures show density and molar volume comparisons (Fig 1), excess volume trends (Fig 2), static structure factor for Ti (Fig 3), coordination numbers and molar volume (Fig 5), radial distribution function peak positions (Fig 6), structure factor Scc(q) (Fig 7), diffusion coefficients and interdiffusion components (Fig 11), temperature dependence of diffusion (Fig 12), and hydrodynamic radius vs atomic radii (Fig 13).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the ML potential and system, presents structural and thermodynamic validation against experiment, analyzes composition-dependent structural changes, investigates transport properties (viscosity and diffusion), and discusses the breakdown of standard transport models in specific concentration regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
